148 - 'We Are Happy With The Current Service Provider' - How to Overcome Sales Objection - YouTube.mp4
Welcome to the next video in the 21 Day Sales Challenge. Now in this video, you're gonna learn how each of your prospects you're talking to will always have what I call the two truths. How you can use those to your advantage to triple your closing ratio. I would say even more than triple. The average salesperson feels totally dejected if the prospect they're talking to tells them they love the company they're already using or the product or service that they already have. Because why? Because we're always hoping for the opposite response. They don't like them. They're hoping that their potential client will be upset with their existing provider. So that they can win the account, but I'm here to tell you that it does not matter whether they say they love their existing product or service they already have, or whether they're already using a provider.
The reason is I'm here to tell you that most people have what I call two truths. Very few people love or hate a hundred percent of what they do or who they use as a provider. There's always something they don't like or something they'd like to improve. Think about it. How often do you hear people express their undivided attachment to a company or product they're using and later curse the day they ever got involved with it? customer says they like their current provider. The way for you to defuse that is to ask this: and let's take a look at the script here. But just give you an example. Let's just say that you sold financial services. You might say something like this: John, I mean, to me, you know, it sounds like things are going fairly well for you.
I mean, is there anything you would change about your portfolio if you could? I will tell you, in most cases, if you ask this question at the right time, they will completely contradict what they just told you. The seven-figure salesperson knows that usually the first answer will only reflect the protected history of the prospect. And the second answer is the real answer and what the person would want to happen. Demonstrate this with somebody today. Ask them, where do you live? How long have you lived there? Do you like living there? Most people will tell you yes. I'd say 8 out of 10 people, no matter where they live, will say yes. And then ask them this question and see their response. Where would you like to live if you could live anywhere in the world?
Let's ask it again. Where would you like to live, John, if you could completely live anywhere you wanted to in the world? I will tell you 99% of the time you'll get a completely different answer than what they just told you. They'll say something like, 'Oh man, if I could just live in the Bahamas or in Florida or over in Europe or it's my dream to live here.' But they just told you they loved where they lived before. People always have two truths. So let's look at an example of the average salesperson and the seven-figure salesperson and how they respond when the potential customer says they love their current provider and are happy with the service they're already getting. So let's take a look at the script here.
Prospect will say, 'Well, we really like our current provider that we've been using. They've got good premiums and our coverage is excellent.' Let's say that you sell insurance products. Here's what the average salesperson would say. 'Well, Mary, I know how you feel. Others I've talked to have felt the same way.' When I first met them, they found that they could get better insurance through my company. Here the salesperson keeps using the old sales technique, 'feel,' 'felt,' 'found.' You've read it in probably almost every traditional sales manual or book you've ever studied. The problem is, in the new economy, it's just not that effective anymore. It's worn out. Your prospects have had it used on them hundreds of times by every other salesperson trying to sell them something.
So it automatically triggers sales pressure in their mind and sales resistance. Now they view you as just another salesperson trying to stuff your solution down their throat and they don't trust you. So at this point, after the salesperson uses the Phil felt found theory on the prospect, usually they'll say something like this. So Mary, if I could show you how my company's insurance is better than your provider now, wouldn't that make sense for you to switch over? Let's take a look at this. When you use the word 'it,' I could show you. It automatically puts the pressure on you now to prove that yours is better, and the potential customer is feeling even more sales pressure at this point. The funny thing is, the salesperson can also feel that same sales pressure as well.
Prospects might say something like this. Well, I'll tell you what, my wife will be home tonight and I can talk to her about what you're offering or call you back later this week. Why don't you just leave a brochure, and I'll call you back if she's interested? Average salesperson would probably say something like this. Well, wouldn't she want your family to have a better insurance rate? I mean, I'll tell you— I mean, if you would, I'll call my manager and see if he'd be willing to lower the cost a little bit for you. If I got it lowered a bit, could you make a decision tonight? Because after today, our promotion ends for the rest of the year. Do you really think the let me talk to my manager routine works anymore in the post-trust era?
It's dead. Everybody knows it. Do you think they can also feel the sales pressure the average salesperson is putting on them? They do. And then what happens next? They get turned off. They shut the door in your face. They hang up the phone. They leave the meeting. Prospect might say something like this. Like I said, I just need to talk to my wife about it first. Have a great night. Hangs up the phone on the salesperson. now here's what the seven figure sales person asked i want you to pay attention to the difference Prospect says, let's go to the script. Well, we really like our current provider, Jeremy. I mean, they've done an amazing job for us. seven-figure salesperson asked, John, that's not a problem. Just curious, what do you like about them?
Prospect would say something like this. Well, we like how they do X, Y, and Z. Seven-figure salesperson would say something like this. Anything else you like about your current provider, John? Prospect would say, 'Well, yeah, they really do a good job with their customer service or whatever.' Seven-figure salesperson, I would say. So John, I mean, to me it sounds like things are going fairly well for you. I mean, is there anything you would change about your provider if you could? This one question can make you hundreds and hundreds of thousands of dollars, even millions of dollars a year in selling. If you don't ask this question. I will tell you 99% of the time when you ask this question at the right time in the conversation, they will tell you something completely different that they want changed and they don't like.
Prospect might say something like this. Let's look at the script. Well, Jeremy, we really like them, but lately we've noticed that they're increasing their monthly cost to us by about 10% to 12%, and it kind of has us concerned. Seven-figure salesperson would ask, 'Well, what do you mean by concerned? Why would we ask that there? We ask that because we don't know what he means. We might have our interpretation of what concern means, but we don't understand unless we ask under-the-surface questions.' Deeper questions and more importantly than us getting those answers, who is starting to tell themselves, who is starting to persuade themselves that they don't like their current provider? Your prospect is. So let's go back to that script. John, what do you mean by concerned?
The prospect might say, well, you know, we're trying to cut our costs around here. A seven-figure salesman might ask. Is there anything you would change about them if you could? The prospect might say, well, we've talked about, you know, geez, their growth, they're becoming a really big company. We just don't feel like we're getting the same service that we got before. Notice they just told you a few things they don't like and that they would want to change. Now you're going to continue asking them questions on those issues that they're having, why they're having them, and how it's affecting them and their company or family, and how important it is for them to change their situation. That is what the seven-figure salesperson asks. Now, I want to thank you for watching this video about learning how each of your prospects you're talking to will always have what I call the two truths, and how you can use that to your advantage to close more sales. Now, the next video we're gonna go over what to say and more importantly, when your potential customer throws out the question, 'Why should I go with you
